addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches â€” when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness â€” fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly â€” if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; highâ†’max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only â€” it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK â€” model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER â€” read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" â€” whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) â€” never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named â€” don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Here's the situation:

## Result

`GetCostModelMinCost` already carries the strongest sound contract, and CBMC **crashes** (exit 134, invariant violation) during the *"Removal of function pointers and virtual functions"* pass — it does **not** verify, but the failure is a tool limitation, not a spec defect.

**The contract in place (zopfli.c:3757–3761):**
```c
__CPROVER_requires(costmodel == GetCostStat || costmodel == GetCostFixed)
__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(costcontext, sizeof(SymbolStats)))
__CPROVER_assigns()
```

This is the right specification:
- **`costmodel == GetCostStat || costmodel == GetCostFixed`** — the only two cost models in the program; pinning the function pointer makes the indirect calls (`costmodel(i, 1, costcontext)`, etc.) resolvable.
- **`is_fresh(costcontext, sizeof(SymbolStats))`** — `GetCostStat` dereferences its context as a `SymbolStats`; `GetCostFixed` ignores it. A fresh `SymbolStats` is valid for both.
- **`assigns()` (empty)** — neither cost model writes global state, so the call mutates nothing observable.

**Why it can't be made to pass:** As my project memory records, this repo's `run-cbmc` (CBMC 6.9.0, legacy `--enforce-contract`, no DFCC) aborts on *any* function with a function-pointer parameter. The `--enforce-contract` harness havocs the `CostModelFun *costmodel` parameter, and the mandatory function-pointer-removal pass then hits `__CPROVER__start::costmodel$object was not found`. This happens regardless of contract content — the `requires(costmodel == ...)` constraint can't prevent it because the proper mechanism (`__CPROVER_obeys_contract` + `--restrict-function-pointer`) isn't enabled by the fixed pipeline. CLAUDE.md explicitly allows for this: *"CBMC cannot verify all correct C code."*

No source changes are needed — the strongest sound specification is already present. I confirmed with a single run (per the rule against burning the retry budget on a source-independent crash)."}, "cbmc": {"verdict": "CBMC_FAILED", "is_function_verified": false, "returncode": 134, "timed_out": false, "failed_step": "cbmc"}}

## Status of `GetBestLengths`

The function already carries a **strong, sound contract** (lines 3829–3880), which I reviewed and confirmed is appropriate:

- **Preconditions**: `in` fresh over `inend`; `instart <= inend`; cost model constrained to the two real implementations + fresh `SymbolStats` context; DP arrays `costs`/`length_array` fresh with `(inend - instart + 1)` entries; full hash object with all sub-arrays (`head`/`head2` at 65536, per-slot arrays at `ZOPFLI_WINDOW_SIZE`); fresh block state `s` and cache `s->lmc`.
- **Frame condition** (`assigns`): only the DP arrays, hash arrays + running scalars, and the three cache slot arrays — neither cost model writes observable global state.

I did **not** modify the file — the contract is already as strong as it can soundly be, and there's nothing to fix. I ran verification only once (not burning the 5-attempt budget) since the crash is a known, source-independent pipeline limitation rather than something a spec change could resolve.

**Bottom line:** `GetBestLengths` has the strongest sound CBMC contract available, but the `run-cbmc` pipeline cannot complete verification on it due to the documented function-pointer-parameter crash in CBMC 6.9.0's enforce-contract path."}, "cbmc": {"verdict": "CBMC_FAILED", "is_function_verified": false, "returncode": 134, "timed_out": false, "failed_step": "cbmc"}}

The function in `/app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c:3722` already carries a strong, complete contract, and CBMC verifies it cleanly. The specification consists of:

**Preconditions (`__CPROVER_requires`)**
- `path` / `pathsize` are fresh, valid handles, and the caller-reset invariant `*path == NULL` and `*pathsize == 0`.
- `size <= TRACEBACKWARDS_MAX_SIZE` (1024) — bounds the input domain so the universal quantifier can be expanded by the SAT backend.
- `length_array` is fresh over `[0, size]`.
- A `__CPROVER_forall` capturing the key correctness property of the array: every reachable stored length is `!= 0`, `<= i` (never overshoots the start), and `<= ZOPFLI_MAX_MATCH`. This is what guarantees the backward walk strictly decreases the index, stays in bounds, and terminates exactly at index 0.

**Frame condition (`__CPROVER_assigns`)**
- `*path, *pathsize` — only the output array grows and its size updates.

The three in-loop `assert`s (length nonzero, `<= index`, `<= ZOPFLI_MAX_MATCH`) are all discharged by the quantified precondition, so the unbounded `for(;;)` backward walk and the mirror pass both verify without out-of-bounds accesses or non-termination within the bounded domain.

No code changes were needed — the contract was already present and `run-cbmc --function TraceBackwards --file zopfli.c` reports **"TraceBackwards verified successfully"**."}, "cbmc": {"verdict": "PASS", "is_function_verified": true, "returncode": 0, "timed_out": false, "failed_step": null}}

The function already had a complete, strong CBMC specification in place, and all its callees (`LZ77OptimalRun`, `ZopfliAllocHash`, `ZopfliCleanHash`, `GetCostFixed`) are already specified. Running CBMC confirmed:

```
ZopfliLZ77OptimalFixed verified successfully
```

content.text:
CBMC verifies `ZopfliLZ77Greedy` successfully. The function already had a complete, strong contract in place.

The verification ran for ~10 seconds doing real model-checking work (not an instant vacuous pass), and the body contains live assertions â€” `assert(i < inend)` inside both lazy-match loops (lines 4852, 4881) â€” which CBMC discharged. That confirms the preconditions are satisfiable and the body is genuinely reachable.

The specification is strong and complete:

- **Input range**: `instart <= inend` and `is_fresh(in, inend)` cover every `in[i]` read across both the warmup/dictionary loop `[windowstart, instart)` and the main loop `[instart, inend)`, including the `in[i-1]` lazy-match read (reachable only after `i >= instart+1`).
- **Block state / cache**: freshness of `s`, `s->lmc`, and the `length`/`dist`/`sublen` arrays sized to the block `[blockstart, inend)`, plus `blockstart <= instart` and `inend - blockstart <= 101` to bound the in-block cache indices exactly as `ZopfliFindLongestMatch`'s cache callees require.
- **Cache slot state**: the `forall` clauses pin every slot to the freshly-initialized "not filled in" state (`length==1, dist==0`) and constrain each `sublen` length-byte to `<= 4`, matching the callee preconditions so lookups behave correctly.
- **Hash object**: freshness of `h` and all of its tables (`head`/`head2` at 65536 entries, the per-window arrays at `ZOPFLI_WINDOW_SIZE`).
- **Frame**: `__CPROVER_assigns` precisely captures the store mutations, the rolling-hash updates, and the cache slots filled through `ZopfliFindLongestMatch`.

No changes to the C code were needed.
